stem:
A 22-month-old boy is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by his parents with 36 hours of watery diarrhoea (8 episodes) and 6 episodes of non-bilious vomiting, last vomit 90 minutes ago. He is alert but irritable and refuses solid food, though he accepted 50 mL of water at triage without further vomiting. There has been no blood in the stool, no fever above 38.5, and no travel.

Two children at his childcare centre have similar symptoms. He has had normal wet nappies but fewer in the past 12 hours. Capillary refill is 2 seconds and skin turgor is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 37.8
data_table.Examination: Alert and irritable, dry mouth, no sunken eyes, normal turgor, soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Not performed; clinical assessment suggests 3 to 5% dehydration

lead_in: What is the most appropriate rehydration strategy?

options.text: Trial oral rehydration solution at 50 mL per kg over 4 hours given in small frequent sips with reassessment
options.rationale: Mild to moderate dehydration is managed with oral rehydration solution at 50 mL per kg over 4 hours in frequent small volumes; clinical reassessment guides escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Insert a peripheral cannula and infuse 0.9% saline at 100 mL per kg over 6 hours empirically for dehydration
options.rationale: IV rehydration is reserved for failed oral or nasogastric trial, shock, persistent vomiting, or severe dehydration; jumping to IV violates the AU stepwise approach. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Commence a single intravenous bolus of 20 mL per kg 0.9% saline and recheck electrolytes in 2 hours
options.rationale: Bolusing without first trialling oral or nasogastric ORS in a non-shocked child is not the AU first step and exposes the child to cannulation unnecessarily. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Administer ondansetron orodispersible 4 mg and reassess feeding tolerance, then admit overnight irrespective of response
options.rationale: Ondansetron is reasonable for persistent vomiting, but routine admission irrespective of response is unnecessary if oral intake resumes; AU guidance favours discharge with ORS once tolerance returns. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Withhold fluids and food for 6 hours to rest the gut, then trial dilute apple juice as the first rehydration agent
options.rationale: Gut rest is not recommended in paediatric gastroenteritis; dilute apple juice has slightly higher relapse compared with structured ORS in moderate cases. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: Acute viral gastroenteritis is one of the commonest paediatric ED presentations. AU paediatric guidance recommends oral rehydration solution as first-line for mild to moderate dehydration, delivered as small frequent volumes totalling around 50 mL per kg over 4 hours, with clinical reassessment. Nasogastric ORS is preferred over intravenous fluid if oral intake fails, as IV access is more invasive and not superior. Antiemetics are not routine; ondansetron is reasonable when vomiting prevents ORS uptake.
key_points: ORS 50 mL/kg over 4 hours is first-line for mild to moderate dehydration
Nasogastric ORS precedes IV fluid in failed oral trials
Single dose ondansetron may rescue persistent vomiting
Gut rest and dilute juice are not routine
explanation.pearls: RCH CPG: 50 mL per kg ORS over 4 hours in frequent small volumes is the AU paediatric standard for mild to moderate dehydration.

stem:
A 7-year-old boy attends a follow-up appointment with his general practitioner after 6 months on inhaled fluticasone 50 mcg twice daily via spacer. Despite reliable adherence (verified by pharmacy refill records), he has needed his salbutamol reliever on 4 days a week and has been waking with cough twice a week. He has had two short courses of oral prednisolone in the past 3 months but no admissions.

There is no smoking in the household, his inhaler technique was reviewed last visit, and his weight and height are on the 50th centile. He has known house dust mite and grass pollen allergy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Chest clear on auscultation; spacer technique reviewed and adequate; no eczema today
data_table.Investigations: Not performed; spirometry FEV1 82% predicted at last visit

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his preventer therapy?

options.text: Continue current fluticasone dose and add montelukast 5 mg nightly as a leukotriene receptor antagonist
options.rationale: Add-on LTRA is an option for partial control but the AU paediatric preferred next step in children with poor control on low-dose ICS is to increase ICS or add LABA above age 5. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook

options.text: Increase fluticasone to 100 mcg twice daily and reassess control and inhaler technique in 4 to 6 weeks
options.rationale: In children with poor symptom control on low-dose ICS the AU preferred step is to double the ICS to medium dose with technique recheck and review at 4 to 6 weeks. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook

options.text: Switch fluticasone to budesonide-formoterol as maintenance and reliever therapy (MART) in this age group
options.rationale: MART is established in adolescents and adults but not the AU preferred approach in children under 12; consider combination ICS-LABA in fixed dosing when escalation is needed. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook

options.text: Stop fluticasone and trial a short course of oral prednisolone followed by environmental allergen avoidance only
options.rationale: Stopping preventer in poorly controlled asthma is unsafe; oral steroid courses do not replace ongoing preventer therapy. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook

options.text: Refer for omalizumab subcutaneous monthly therapy given his allergic phenotype and current symptom burden
options.rationale: Omalizumab is reserved for severe IgE-mediated asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS plus LABA in specialist clinics; this is premature at low-dose ICS. (Source: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook)
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook

explanation.summary: Childhood asthma management follows a stepwise escalation guided by symptom frequency, reliever use, night waking and prior flares. Children under 12 starting at low-dose ICS who remain poorly controlled despite adherence and good technique are stepped up to medium-dose ICS (typically by doubling), with reassessment at 4 to 6 weeks. Add-on LTRA, fixed combination ICS-LABA, and biologics are subsequent options. Salbutamol use 3 or more days a week or night waking once a week or more indicates poor control.
key_points: Low-dose ICS to medium-dose ICS is the preferred first step in children
MART is not routinely used in children under 12 in Australia
Reliever use 3+ days/week or nocturnal cough = poor control
Always recheck technique and adherence before escalation
explanation.pearls: AU Asthma Handbook in children under 12: double low-dose ICS first before MART or biologics; review at 4 to 6 weeks.

sources.label: National Asthma Council Australia: Asthma Handbook

stem: A previously well 6-year-old girl was diagnosed with Henoch Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) 10 days ago after a palpable purpuric rash on the lower limbs, intermittent crampy abdominal pain, and bilateral ankle swelling. At initial review her urinalysis was negative, blood pressure was normotensive, and she had no haematuria or proteinuria. She has been managed at home with paracetamol and her family reports the rash is fading. She has returned today for follow-up and her urinalysis today shows blood 3+ and protein 2+. Her blood pressure is 118/78.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 118/78 (>95th centile for age and height), RR 20, SpO2 99%, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Fading purpura on lower limbs; soft non-tender abdomen; no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick blood 3+ protein 2+; urine protein:creatinine ratio (UPCR) pending; creatinine 38; albumin 36

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her management?

options.text: Reassure the family the rash is resolving and review with urinalysis again in 4 weeks at the next scheduled visit
options.rationale: New significant haematuria and proteinuria with elevated blood pressure require urgent paediatric assessment, not routine follow-up. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 1 mg per kg without specialist consultation and continue urinalysis weekly at home
options.rationale: Steroid initiation in HSP nephritis with proteinuria requires paediatric nephrology input on biopsy and treatment intensity; starting alone is not appropriate. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric nephrology for assessment including UPCR, eGFR estimation and consideration of biopsy
options.rationale: Persistent or new proteinuria with hypertension after HSP requires urgent paediatric nephrology referral; UPCR and eGFR guide need for renal biopsy and immunosuppression. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Recommend an angiotensin receptor blocker via the general practitioner without specialist nephrology input now
options.rationale: RAAS blockade may be used for persistent proteinuria but only after specialist assessment, biopsy where indicated, and confirmation of injury pattern. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

options.text: Admit for intravenous methylprednisolone pulse 30 mg per kg daily for three days irrespective of biopsy result
options.rationale: Pulse methylprednisolone is reserved for biopsy-proven severe crescentic nephritis or rapidly progressive disease, not initial assessment of significant urinalysis change. (Source: RCH CPG: HSP)

explanation.summary: Henoch Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) typically affects children under 10 with a triad of purpura, arthritis and abdominal pain; renal involvement develops in around a third and largely declares within 6 months. AU paediatric guidance is weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for the first month, fortnightly to 3 months, and monthly to 6 months. New or persistent proteinuria, hypertension, or rising creatinine triggers urgent paediatric nephrology referral for UPCR, eGFR, and biopsy consideration. Long-term proteinuria and hypertension are managed with RAAS blockade.
key_points: Surveillance urinalysis and BP for 6 months catches late nephritis
New proteinuria + hypertension = urgent nephrology referral
Biopsy indications include crescentic disease and persistent significant proteinuria
Routine steroids do not prevent nephritis
explanation.pearls: Urinalysis surveillance for 6 months and a urine protein:creatinine ratio above 100 mg per mmol prompt urgent paediatric nephrology referral in HSP.

stem:
A 14-year-old boy presents to a regional emergency department on day 4 of a sore throat originally treated with phenoxymethylpenicillin by his general practitioner 48 hours ago after a positive throat swab for group A Streptococcus. Over the past 12 hours his throat pain has become severe and predominantly right-sided; he has trouble swallowing his own saliva and his voice has become muffled.

He has no stridor, no drooling whilst sitting upright, and no neck stiffness. On examination, he has trismus opening only 2 cm, deviation of the uvula to the left, and a fluctuant bulge on the right superior tonsillar pole.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112, BP 118/72, RR 20, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 38.6
data_table.Examination: Trismus 2 cm, uvula deviated to the left, fluctuant right superior tonsillar pole; no stridor
data_table.Investigations: Bedside ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection 2.5 cm at right tonsillar pole

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Switch from phenoxymethylpenicillin to oral clindamycin and continue review with the general practitioner tomorrow
options.rationale: Established peritonsillar abscess requires drainage in addition to antibiotics; antibiotics alone rarely resolve a fluctuant collection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Arrange contrast-enhanced CT neck to confirm abscess before any drainage despite confirmatory ultrasound findings
options.rationale: Imaging is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty or suspected deeper space involvement; ultrasound is already confirmatory and CT delays definitive drainage. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Arrange urgent tonsillectomy under general anaesthesia within 24 hours as definitive management in adolescents
options.rationale: Acute tonsillectomy is not first-line; needle aspiration or incision and drainage with intravenous antibiotics is the AU first step. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer urgently for needle aspiration or incision and drainage with intravenous benzylpenicillin and metronidazole
options.rationale: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) requires needle aspiration or incision and drainage by an ENT or experienced ED clinician, plus intravenous benzylpenicillin and metronidazole pending culture. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence high-dose dexamethasone alone without drainage to reduce swelling and reassess at 24 hours
options.rationale: Steroids are an adjunct to drainage and antibiotics, not a substitute; relying on steroids alone risks airway compromise as the abscess enlarges. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Peritonsillar abscess (quinsy) is a complication of bacterial tonsillitis classically presenting with trismus, muffled voice, severe unilateral throat pain and uvular deviation. AU first-line management combines drainage (needle aspiration or incision and drainage) with intravenous benzylpenicillin plus metronidazole to cover oral anaerobes, switching to oral therapy once afebrile. Dexamethasone may be added to reduce pain and swelling. Tonsillectomy is reserved for recurrent disease or as an acute Quinsy tonsillectomy in selected cases. Airway compromise (drooling, stridor) is the cue to mobilise anaesthesia and ENT immediately.
key_points: Trismus + muffled voice + uvular deviation = peritonsillar abscess
Drainage + IV benzylpenicillin + metronidazole is first-line
Steroids are adjunctive, not substitutive
CT neck is reserved for deeper space concerns
explanation.pearls: Quinsy: IV benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6 hourly plus metronidazole 500 mg 8 hourly, with bedside drainage by experienced ED or ENT clinician.

stem: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the general practitioner with worsening eczema flares for 3 months affecting her flexures, face and trunk. Her parents are applying QV cream once daily but have stopped using hydrocortisone 1% after reading online about steroid concerns. She is itchy enough to wake at night and has new excoriations across the antecubital fossae. There is no oozing, no honey-coloured crusting, and no systemic upset. She is fully vaccinated and is otherwise growing well. There is a strong atopic family history including asthma and allergic rhinitis.

data_table.Examination: Diffuse erythema and lichenification in flexures; excoriations; no impetiginisation; no eyelid swelling

lead_in: What is the most appropriate skin care plan?

options.text: Continue QV cream only and avoid all topical steroids; trial a hypoallergenic diet for 6 weeks instead
options.rationale: Dietary elimination is not first-line in eczema without IgE-confirmed food trigger and skipping topical steroid undertreats inflammation, perpetuating itch and infection risk. (Source: ASCIA Eczema Action Plan)

options.text: Switch hydrocortisone for a topical calcineurin inhibitor as first-line therapy for paediatric flexural eczema
options.rationale: Topical calcineurin inhibitors are second-line steroid-sparing agents; for flare control in a steroid-naive flexural eczema, an appropriate-potency topical corticosteroid is first. (Source: ASCIA Eczema Action Plan)

options.text: Wet-wrap a high-potency topical steroid (mometasone furoate) twice daily across all affected areas for 4 weeks
options.rationale: High-potency steroids and wet wraps are reserved for severe or refractory disease; an appropriate-potency step is preferred and wet wrap is short-term only. (Source: ASCIA Eczema Action Plan)

options.text: Commence oral antihistamine cetirizine nightly and topical antibiotic mupirocin to all eczematous skin
options.rationale: Antihistamines do not treat the underlying inflammation; topical antibiotics are reserved for impetiginised eczema, not routine flares. (Source: ASCIA Eczema Action Plan)

options.text: Educate family on regular emollients with methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% to affected areas once daily for 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: AU paediatric guidance recommends thick emollients applied liberally plus a moderate-potency topical steroid such as methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% once daily to the flare areas for 1 to 2 weeks, with education to address steroid phobia. (Source: ASCIA Eczema Action Plan)

explanation.summary: Paediatric atopic eczema is managed primarily with emollients applied liberally and topical corticosteroids matched to severity and site. Steroid phobia is a major barrier and education on safe potency-by-site (mild for face, moderate to potent for body), the fingertip unit, and duration is essential. Calcineurin inhibitors are second-line steroid-sparing for face/eyelids or maintenance. Bleach baths and intermittent proactive twice-weekly steroid application reduce flare recurrence in moderate disease. Systemic therapy and biologics are specialist domains.
key_points: Emollient + appropriate-potency steroid is first-line in flares
Moderate potency (methylprednisolone aceponate) suits non-facial flexural eczema
Topical calcineurin inhibitors are second-line steroid-sparing agents
Antihistamines and routine antibiotics have no flare-control role
explanation.pearls: ASCIA Eczema Action Plan supports methylprednisolone aceponate 0.1% once daily for 1 to 2 weeks for moderate flexural flare, alongside generous emollient.

subtopic_detail: Valproate in women of childbearing potential
stem: A 26-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder is reviewed in the community mental health clinic. She has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 18 months following two manic episodes and remains in remission. She is in a stable relationship and is considering pregnancy in the next 12 months. She has no current depressive symptoms, no thyroid disease, and no recent admissions. Her general practitioner recently issued repeat scripts without discussing pregnancy planning. She is not on any reliable contraception currently and reports inconsistent condom use.

data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic; mental state stable; no involuntary movements
data_table.Investigations: Sodium valproate level 480 micromol/L (within range); LFTs normal; pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discuss the major risks of valproate in pregnancy, ensure highly effective contraception, and plan a cross-taper to lithium or lamotrigine
options.rationale: Valproate carries a high risk of neural tube defects and neurodevelopmental impairment; AU guidance recommends avoidance in women of childbearing potential without highly effective contraception and pre-pregnancy switching to lithium or lamotrigine where clinically possible. (Source: AMH; RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

options.text: Continue valproate at the current dose and add high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily for pregnancy prophylaxis
options.rationale: Folic acid 5 mg does not mitigate the neurodevelopmental risks of valproate and continuing without contraception or switch plan is not appropriate. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Stop valproate abruptly today and start sertraline 50 mg as a safer alternative mood-stabiliser before pregnancy
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation risks relapse; SSRIs are antidepressants, not mood stabilisers, and inadequate for bipolar maintenance. (Source: RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

options.text: Continue valproate and reassure her that the teratogenic risk is largely confined to the first trimester only
options.rationale: Valproate carries dose-dependent first-trimester structural and longer-term neurodevelopmental risk; framing it as trimester-limited misrepresents AU evidence. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Refer to a perinatal psychiatry service and continue valproate unchanged pending tertiary review in 3 months
options.rationale: Perinatal referral is appropriate but does not justify continuing valproate without contraception in the interim given the magnitude of teratogenic risk. (Source: RANZCP CPG Mood Disorders)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is highly teratogenic, with foetal valproate syndrome including neural tube defects, dysmorphism, congenital heart disease, and significant neurodevelopmental impairment dose-dependently. AU prescribing guidance requires women of childbearing potential to be on highly effective contraception and within a pregnancy prevention framework; pre-pregnancy planning involves cross-tapering to lithium or lamotrigine where clinically feasible, with perinatal psychiatry input. Sudden discontinuation risks relapse, so transitions are gradual with overlap. Folic acid 5 mg helps but does not negate valproate risk.
key_points: Valproate carries high teratogenic risk: NTDs and neurodevelopmental impairment
Highly effective contraception is required for any prescribing in women of childbearing potential
Pre-pregnancy switch to lithium or lamotrigine is preferred where clinically feasible
Folic acid alone does not mitigate valproate risk
explanation.pearls: AMH guidance: valproate prescribing in women of childbearing potential requires pregnancy prevention plus active plan for switch to lithium or lamotrigine before conception.

stem: A 34-year-old man attends his general practice seeking assessment for adult attention deficit hyperactivity disorder. He reports lifelong difficulty completing tasks, frequent job changes due to disorganisation, and being told he was disruptive at primary school. School reports from year 4 confirm restlessness and inattention. He scored highly on a self-administered Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS-v1.1). He has a remote past of cannabis use (ceased 5 years ago), no current substance use, no current depressive episode, and is otherwise medically well. He has no history of cardiac disease and his resting ECG is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 70, BP 122/78, RR 14, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Engaged, fidgety, no thought disorder; affect bright; school reports support childhood symptoms
data_table.Investigations: ASRS-v1.1 part A positive (6/6); ECG sinus rhythm; thyroid function normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in his diagnostic pathway?

options.text: Commence a trial of methylphenidate immediate-release 10 mg three times daily for 4 weeks and reassess response
options.rationale: Adult ADHD prescribing of stimulants in Australia is restricted to specialist initiation under state-based authority arrangements; GP-initiated trial is not permitted. (Source: RANZCP CPG: ADHD; State Health Department Stimulant Approvals)

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist or paediatrician with adult ADHD experience for diagnostic assessment and treatment authority
options.rationale: Adult ADHD in Australia is diagnosed and treated by a specialist (psychiatrist or paediatrician with adult experience) who can obtain the state-based stimulant approval; GPs may then continue prescribing under shared-care arrangements. (Source: RANZCP CPG: ADHD)

options.text: Refer for neuropsychological testing as a prerequisite to any specialist clinical assessment for adult ADHD
options.rationale: Neuropsychological testing supports complex cases but is not a prerequisite; AU guidance is clinical diagnosis based on history, collateral, and DSM-5 criteria. (Source: RANZCP CPG: ADHD)

options.text: Provide reassurance, cognitive behavioural therapy referral, and reassess in 12 months without diagnostic workup
options.rationale: Established functional impairment and a positive screen warrant specialist assessment; deferring CBT alone without diagnosis is inadequate. (Source: RANZCP CPG: ADHD)

options.text: Prescribe atomoxetine 40 mg daily as a non-stimulant alternative bypassing the need for specialist diagnostic assessment
options.rationale: Atomoxetine still requires the same specialist diagnostic assessment as stimulants; non-stimulant choice does not bypass diagnostic pathway. (Source: RANZCP CPG: ADHD)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis in Australia requires DSM-5-TR criteria fulfilment with childhood-onset features, collateral history, functional impairment in two or more domains, and exclusion of alternative explanations. Pharmacological treatment is specialist-initiated: psychostimulants (methylphenidate, dexamfetamine, lisdexamfetamine) require state-based authority approvals, while atomoxetine and guanfacine are non-stimulant alternatives. Once stable, GPs may continue prescribing under shared-care arrangements. Driving safety, cardiovascular history, and substance misuse are formal risk-assessment domains.
key_points: AU adult ADHD requires specialist (psychiatrist or paediatrician) diagnosis and treatment initiation
Stimulants require state-based authority
Childhood-onset features must be documented
Cardiovascular and driving risks must be assessed before stimulant initiation
explanation.pearls: AU adult ADHD: specialist (psychiatrist or paediatrician) initiates stimulants under state-based authority; GPs continue under shared care.

sources.label: AADPA Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem:
A 56-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with 24 hours of escalating left lower-leg pain, warmth, and a poorly demarcated erythematous patch above the medial malleolus. He is now systemically unwell with rigors. His pain is markedly out of proportion to the visual changes and he describes a numb central zone. There are no obvious skin breaks, no bullae yet, and no necrosis on inspection.

He has not travelled and has not had recent saltwater exposure. He has not had surgery. He uses metformin and atorvastatin and has no known allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, BP 92/56, RR 26, SpO2 95% room air, Temp 39.2
data_table.Examination: Erythema ill-defined; severe pain on light palpation; central anaesthesia; no crepitus; no bullae yet
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4, Na 132, glucose 18, creatinine 158, CRP 320, Hb 132, lactate 3.4; LRINEC 9

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily and outpatient review in 24 hours for a moderate cellulitis
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, systemic toxicity and an LRINEC of 9 indicate possible necrotising soft tissue infection; oral therapy and outpatient review is unsafe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit for intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g 6 hourly with limb elevation and re-image at 24 hours
options.rationale: IV flucloxacillin alone does not cover the polymicrobial mix typical of necrotising fasciitis; delaying surgery to await imaging worsens outcome. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Activate immediate surgical review for debridement, give intravenous meropenem plus clindamycin, and resuscitate with fluids
options.rationale: An LRINEC score of 6 or more (here 9) combined with pain out of proportion, systemic toxicity and central anaesthesia is highly suggestive of necrotising fasciitis; AU practice mandates urgent surgical exploration with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (meropenem) plus clindamycin for toxin suppression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Order an urgent MRI of the lower limb and defer surgical consultation until imaging confirms fascial involvement
options.rationale: MRI may have a role in stable patients but should not delay surgical exploration when clinical features and LRINEC strongly suggest necrotising fasciitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence intravenous vancomycin alone empirically pending culture results from initial blood cultures and skin swab
options.rationale: Single agent vancomycin under-covers anaerobes and gram negatives that contribute to type I necrotising fasciitis; broad cover plus clindamycin is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infections classically present with pain out of proportion to visible changes, systemic toxicity, and rapidly evolving skin findings (bullae, anaesthesia, crepitus). The LRINEC score combines CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine, and glucose to support recognition; a score of 6 or more is suspicious and 8 or more is high risk, although clinical judgement overrides the score. AU first-line empirical therapy is broad-spectrum cover with meropenem (or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus clindamycin for toxin suppression and vancomycin if MRSA risk; urgent surgical debridement is the definitive intervention.
key_points: Pain out of proportion + systemic toxicity + LRINEC >=6 = act on suspicion
Empirical: meropenem (or pip-taz) + clindamycin + vancomycin (MRSA risk)
Urgent surgical debridement is the definitive therapy
Imaging must not delay theatre when clinical suspicion is high
explanation.pearls: LRINEC score above 6 with pain out of proportion warrants urgent surgical exploration plus meropenem + clindamycin in Australian sepsis pathways.

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is brought to a general practice clinic with pallor noted by her grandmother and reduced energy at playgroup over 6 weeks. She drinks 800 mL of cow's milk daily, eats limited solids (mainly white bread and pasta), and has no overt blood loss. She was born at term, exclusively breastfed for the first 4 months, and has been gaining weight on the 25th centile track.

She has had no recurrent infections, no jaundice, and there is no family history of haemoglobinopathy. Examination shows pale conjunctivae and palmar creases but no jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 124, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae; no jaundice; soft non-tender abdomen; no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, MCV 64 fL, MCH 22 pg, ferritin 5 mcg/L, CRP 4, reticulocytes low; faecal occult blood negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Empirical oral antibiotics for occult bacterial infection and recheck full blood count in 4 weeks before iron replacement
options.rationale: There is no clinical evidence of infection and a CRP of 4 is normal; antibiotics are not indicated and delay iron correction. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Recommend intramuscular iron polymaltose injection given likely poor oral adherence in toddlers under 2 years
options.rationale: Parenteral iron is reserved for failed oral therapy or malabsorption; oral elemental iron is first-line in most paediatric cases with dietary modification. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric haematology for bone marrow biopsy and haemoglobinopathy electrophoresis screen
options.rationale: Classic dietary iron deficiency anaemia in a toddler with high milk intake and no red flags does not require haematology referral or marrow biopsy. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Start oral elemental iron 3 mg per kg daily, reduce cow's milk to under 500 mL daily, and recheck haemoglobin at 6 weeks
options.rationale: First-line treatment for paediatric iron deficiency anaemia is oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg per kg daily combined with dietary advice to limit cow's milk below 500 mL per day; recheck haemoglobin and ferritin at 4 to 6 weeks. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

options.text: Transfuse packed red cells now as a one-off given the symptomatic anaemia, then commence ongoing oral supplementation
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia with cardiovascular compromise; this child is haemodynamically stable and responds to oral iron. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron Deficiency)

explanation.summary: Toddler iron deficiency anaemia frequently reflects excess cow's milk intake (over 600 mL daily reduces iron absorption and displaces iron-rich solids), low dietary iron, and ongoing physiological demand. AU paediatric guidance is oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg per kg daily for 3 months alongside dietary modification (cow's milk under 500 mL per day, iron-rich foods, vitamin C-rich drinks); recheck haemoglobin and ferritin at 4 to 6 weeks. Parenteral iron, transfusion and haematology referral are reserved for failures or red flags. Screening also covers maternal mental health and developmental milestones since chronic iron deficiency affects neurodevelopment.
key_points: Oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day is first-line
Cow's milk under 500 mL/day reduces ongoing displacement of iron-rich solids
Recheck Hb and ferritin at 4 to 6 weeks
Continue iron 3 months after Hb normalises to replete stores
explanation.pearls: RCH guidance: oral iron 3 to 6 mg per kg per day plus cow's milk under 500 mL per day; recheck Hb and ferritin at 4 to 6 weeks.

stem:
A 13-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with 4 weeks of gradually worsening left groin and knee pain after no specific injury. His parents note that he has started walking with the left foot turned outwards. He has gained 12 kg in the past year; his body mass index is now on the 97th centile. There is no fever, no rash, no morning stiffness, and no other joint involvement.

His mother has type 2 diabetes. On examination, his left leg is held in slight external rotation; passive hip flexion causes obligate external rotation; he can weight-bear with a limp but not stand on the left leg alone without pain.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, BP 118/74, RR 16, SpO2 99% room air, Temp 36.7
data_table.Examination: Left leg externally rotated; obligate external rotation on hip flexion; antalgic limp; no joint effusion of knee
data_table.Investigations: AP and frog-leg lateral pelvis radiographs available; CRP 6, WCC 8.2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with simple analgesia and outpatient physiotherapy review in 1 week for an irritable hip diagnosis
options.rationale: Irritable hip rarely affects adolescents over 10 and typically resolves quickly; the obligate external rotation sign and risk profile make this presentation surgically urgent. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

options.text: Recommend a low-impact exercise programme with weight loss advice and rheumatology review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Active orthopaedic pathology with mechanical signs needs prompt surgical evaluation rather than deferred rheumatology assessment. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

options.text: Order a bone scan to characterise the lesion before any orthopaedic referral or weight-bearing restrictions
options.rationale: Plain radiographs (AP and frog-leg lateral) are the imaging of choice; bone scan is not first-line and delays definitive surgical fixation. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

options.text: Aspirate the hip under ultrasound guidance to exclude septic arthritis before further definitive imaging or referral
options.rationale: Aspiration is indicated for suspected septic arthritis; this presentation is not febrile, CRP is low, and the obligate external rotation sign supports a mechanical aetiology. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

options.text: Make the patient strictly non-weight-bearing, refer for urgent orthopaedic review with AP and frog-leg lateral radiographs
options.rationale: Obligate external rotation in an overweight adolescent with chronic groin pain referred to the knee is slipped upper femoral epiphysis until proven otherwise; non-weight bearing and urgent orthopaedic referral with AP and frog-leg lateral views is the AU standard to prevent slip progression and avascular necrosis. (Source: RCH CPG: SUFE)

explanation.summary: Slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE) typically presents in obese adolescents between 10 and 15 years with chronic groin or referred knee pain and obligate external rotation on hip flexion. Bilateral disease occurs in up to 40% and risk factors include obesity and endocrine conditions (hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency). Definitive management is urgent in situ pinning to prevent slip progression and avascular necrosis; the child is kept strictly non-weight bearing until surgery. Perthes disease affects younger children (4 to 10) and has a different radiographic pattern.
key_points: Obligate external rotation on hip flexion in an adolescent = SUFE until proven otherwise
Pain may refer to the knee and delay diagnosis
Make strictly non-weight bearing; urgent orthopaedic referral; AP + frog-leg lateral views
Up to 40% of cases are bilateral over time
explanation.pearls: Obligate external rotation in adolescent hip flexion is the cardinal SUFE sign; AP + frog-leg lateral X-rays confirm and surgery prevents progression.
